mortgage-payment-calculator.q: What is a good mortgage interest rate?
mortgage-payment-calculator.a: A good mortgage rate is typically 0.5-1% below the national average. As of 2026, rates between 6-7% are common for 30-year fixed mortgages. Your actual rate depends on credit score (740+ gets the best rates), down payment size, loan type, and market conditions.

mortgage-payment-calculator.q: How much house can I afford on a $100,000 salary?
mortgage-payment-calculator.a: Using the 28/36 rule, your mortgage payment shouldn't exceed 28% of gross income. On $100,000/year, that's about $2,333/month for housing. At 6.5% interest with 20% down, this typically supports a home price of $350,000-$420,000 depending on taxes and insurance in your area.

mortgage-payment-calculator.q: Should I get a 15-year or 30-year mortgage?
mortgage-payment-calculator.a: A 15-year mortgage has higher monthly payments but saves 50-60% in total interest and builds equity faster. Choose 15-year if you can comfortably afford the higher payment. Choose 30-year for lower monthly obligations and more cash flow flexibility — you can always make extra payments.

mortgage-payment-calculator.q: What is PMI and how do I avoid it?
mortgage-payment-calculator.a: Private Mortgage Insurance (PMI) is required when your down payment is less than 20%. It typically costs 0.5-1.5% of the loan amount annually ($50-$150/month on a $200k loan). To avoid it: put 20% down, use a VA loan (no PMI required), or ask about lender-paid PMI options.

compound-interest-calculator.q: What is compound interest and how does it work?
compound-interest-calculator.a: Compound interest is interest earned on both your original investment AND previously earned interest. Unlike simple interest (earned only on principal), compound interest creates exponential growth. For example, $10,000 at 7% compound interest becomes $19,672 in 10 years vs $17,000 with simple interest.

compound-interest-calculator.q: How long does it take to double your money with compound interest?
compound-interest-calculator.a: Use the Rule of 72: divide 72 by your annual interest rate. At 7% returns, money doubles in ~10.3 years. At 10% (stock market average), it doubles in ~7.2 years. At 4% (savings account), it takes ~18 years. This assumes reinvesting all returns.

compound-interest-calculator.q: How much should I invest monthly to become a millionaire?
compound-interest-calculator.a: At 10% average annual returns (S&P 500 historical average): invest $500/month for 30 years ($1.13M), $1,000/month for 25 years ($1.18M), or $2,000/month for 20 years ($1.53M). Starting earlier dramatically reduces how much you need to contribute each month.

compound-interest-calculator.q: Is daily or monthly compounding better?
compound-interest-calculator.a: Daily compounding earns slightly more than monthly, but the difference is minimal. $10,000 at 5% for 10 years: daily compounding = $16,487, monthly = $16,470 — a difference of only $17. Focus on the interest rate and time period rather than compounding frequency.

bmi-calculator.q: What is a healthy BMI for adults?
bmi-calculator.a: A healthy BMI is between 18.5 and 24.9. Below 18.5 is underweight, 25-29.9 is overweight, and 30+ is obese. However, BMI doesn't account for muscle mass, bone density, age, or fat distribution — a muscular athlete may have a 'high' BMI but excellent health.

bmi-calculator.q: Is BMI accurate for athletes and muscular people?
bmi-calculator.a: No, BMI is less accurate for athletes, bodybuilders, and very muscular individuals. Muscle weighs more than fat, so muscular people often have 'overweight' BMIs despite low body fat. For these individuals, body fat percentage, waist circumference, or DEXA scans provide better health assessments.

bmi-calculator.q: How do I lower my BMI safely?
bmi-calculator.a: To lower BMI safely, aim for 1-2 pounds of weight loss per week through a moderate calorie deficit (500-750 calories/day below maintenance) combined with regular exercise. Focus on whole foods, lean protein, and strength training to preserve muscle. Consult a healthcare provider before starting any weight loss plan.

calorie-calculator.q: How many calories should I eat per day to lose weight?
calorie-calculator.a: For safe weight loss of 1 lb/week, eat 500 calories below your TDEE (Total Daily Energy Expenditure). For most adults, this means 1,500-2,000 calories/day for women and 2,000-2,500 for men, depending on activity level. Never go below 1,200 (women) or 1,500 (men) without medical supervision.

calorie-calculator.q: What is TDEE and why does it matter?
calorie-calculator.a: TDEE (Total Daily Energy Expenditure) is the total calories your body burns daily including basal metabolism, digestion, and physical activity. It matters because eating above TDEE causes weight gain, eating below causes weight loss, and eating at TDEE maintains weight. It's the foundation of any nutrition plan.

calorie-calculator.q: Does exercise increase how many calories I can eat?
calorie-calculator.a: Yes, exercise increases your TDEE, allowing more food intake while maintaining or losing weight. However, most people overestimate exercise calories. A 30-minute jog burns approximately 250-350 calories — about one small meal. Combine exercise with mindful eating rather than 'eating back' all exercise calories.

auto-loan-calculator.q: What is a good interest rate for a car loan?
auto-loan-calculator.a: Good car loan rates in 2026: excellent credit (750+) gets 4-6%, good credit (700-749) gets 6-8%, fair credit (650-699) gets 8-12%. New cars typically get lower rates than used cars. Credit unions often offer rates 1-2% lower than dealership financing.

auto-loan-calculator.q: How much car can I afford on my salary?
auto-loan-calculator.a: The 20/4/10 rule is a good guideline: 20% down payment, no more than a 4-year loan, and total car expenses (payment + insurance + gas) under 10% of gross monthly income. On a $60,000 salary, that's about $500/month total, supporting a car in the $20,000-$28,000 range.

auto-loan-calculator.q: Should I choose a longer loan term for lower payments?
auto-loan-calculator.a: Longer terms (72-84 months) reduce monthly payments but cost significantly more in interest and risk being 'underwater' (owing more than the car is worth). A $30,000 car at 6%: 48 months = $704/month, $3,800 interest. 72 months = $497/month, $5,800 interest — $2,000 more for the same car.

tip-calculator.q: How much should you tip at a restaurant?
tip-calculator.a: Standard restaurant tipping in the US: 15% for adequate service, 18-20% for good service, 20-25% for exceptional service. For large parties (6+), many restaurants add automatic 18-20% gratuity. For takeout, 10-15% is appreciated but not expected. Always tip on the pre-tax bill amount.

tip-calculator.q: Do you tip on tax or before tax?
tip-calculator.a: Etiquette experts agree you should tip on the pre-tax subtotal, not the total after tax. For example, on a $50 meal with $4 tax, tip 20% on $50 ($10), not on $54 ($10.80). The difference is small but adds up over time.

tip-calculator.q: How do you split a tip between multiple servers?
tip-calculator.a: When splitting a check, each person should tip 18-20% on their portion. If one person pays the full bill, they should tip on the full pre-tax amount and split the total (bill + tip) evenly. For group dining, rounding up slightly ensures servers aren't shortchanged.

retirement-planning-calculator.q: How much money do I need to retire?
retirement-planning-calculator.a: The common guideline is 25x your annual expenses (the 4% rule). If you spend $50,000/year, you need $1.25 million. If you spend $80,000/year, you need $2 million. This assumes a 4% safe withdrawal rate and a portfolio lasting 30+ years. Adjust upward for early retirement or conservative estimates.

retirement-planning-calculator.q: At what age can I retire?
retirement-planning-calculator.a: You can retire when your investments generate enough income to cover expenses. Social Security starts at 62 (reduced) or 67 (full). To retire earlier, you need more savings. The FIRE movement targets 50-55 by saving 50-70% of income. Most financial advisors suggest having 10-12x your salary saved by age 67.

retirement-planning-calculator.q: How much should I save for retirement each month?
retirement-planning-calculator.a: Save at least 15% of gross income for retirement (including employer match). Starting at 25, that's roughly $375/month on a $60,000 salary. Starting at 35, you need $750/month to catch up. Starting at 45, you'd need $1,500+/month. The earlier you start, the less you need to save monthly due to compound growth.

budget-calculator.a: The 50/30/20 rule allocates after-tax income as: 50% for needs (rent, groceries, insurance, minimum debt payments), 30% for wants (dining out, entertainment, hobbies, subscriptions), and 20% for savings and extra debt payments. On $5,000/month take-home: $2,500 needs, $1,500 wants, $1,000 savings.

budget-calculator.q: How do I stick to a budget?
budget-calculator.a: Key strategies: 1) Track every expense for 30 days to find leaks, 2) Use the envelope method or separate accounts for categories, 3) Automate savings on payday (pay yourself first), 4) Review weekly not just monthly, 5) Build in 'fun money' so the budget doesn't feel restrictive. The best budget is one you'll actually follow.

budget-calculator.q: How much of my income should go to rent?
budget-calculator.a: The standard guideline is spending no more than 30% of gross income on housing (rent + utilities). On $60,000/year ($5,000/month), that's $1,500 for rent. In high-cost cities, many spend 35-40%, but this leaves less for savings and flexibility. If possible, aim for 25% to build wealth faster.

kg-to-lbs.a: 1 kilogram equals 2.20462 pounds. To convert kilograms to pounds, multiply by 2.20462.

celsius-to-fahrenheit.q: How do you convert celsius to fahrenheit?
celsius-to-fahrenheit.a: To convert Celsius to Fahrenheit, multiply by 1.8 (9/5) and add 32. So °F = °C × 9/5 + 32.

celsius-to-fahrenheit.q: What is 1 degree Celsius in fahrenheit?

fahrenheit-to-celsius.q: How do you convert fahrenheit to celsius?
fahrenheit-to-celsius.a: To convert Fahrenheit to Celsius, subtract 32 and multiply by 0.5556 (5/9). So °C = (°F − 32) × 5/9.

sales-tax-calculator.q: How do you calculate sales tax?
sales-tax-calculator.a: Multiply the price by the tax rate as a decimal. Sales tax = Price × (Rate ÷ 100). For a $100 item at 7.25%, the tax is $7.25 and the total is $107.25.

sales-tax-calculator.q: How do I find the price before tax (reverse sales tax)?
sales-tax-calculator.a: Divide the total by 1 plus the tax rate as a decimal: Pre-tax price = Total ÷ (1 + Rate ÷ 100). A $107.25 total at 7.25% works out to $100.00 before tax. Use the calculator's 'Remove tax' mode to do this automatically.

sales-tax-calculator.q: Which states have no sales tax?
sales-tax-calculator.a: Five states have no statewide sales tax: Alaska, Delaware, Montana, New Hampshire, and Oregon. Note that some Alaska localities still charge a local sales tax.

sales-tax-calculator.q: Is the sales tax rate the same everywhere in a state?
sales-tax-calculator.a: No. The state sets a base rate, but counties and cities often add their own sales tax. The combined rate at checkout can be several percent higher than the state base rate, so confirm the rate for your exact location.

sales-tax-calculator.q: What is the highest combined sales tax in the US?
sales-tax-calculator.a: Combined state-plus-local rates can exceed 10% in parts of states like Louisiana, Tennessee, Arkansas, and Washington. California has the highest state base rate at 7.25%.

discount-calculator.q: How do you calculate a discount?
discount-calculator.a: Multiply the original price by (1 − discount ÷ 100). For $80 at 25% off: $80 × 0.75 = $60.00, so you save $20.00.

discount-calculator.q: How do I calculate percent off?
discount-calculator.a: The savings equal Price × (Percent ÷ 100), and the sale price is what's left. 30% off a $50 item saves $15 and costs $35.
Stacked discounts multiply rather than add. '25% off, then an extra 20% off' is 0.75 × 0.80 = 0.60 of the original price — a 40% effective discount, not 45%.

discount-calculator.q: What is the final price after a 25% discount?
discount-calculator.a: The final price is 75% of the original. A $120 item at 25% off costs $90.00, saving you $30.00.

discount-calculator.q: How do I find the original price from a sale price?
discount-calculator.a: Divide the sale price by (1 − discount ÷ 100). If something costs $60 after 25% off, the original was $60 ÷ 0.75 = $80.00.

home-affordability-calculator.q: How much house can I afford on my salary?
home-affordability-calculator.a: A common guideline is the 28/36 rule: spend no more than 28% of gross monthly income on housing and 36% on total debt. On a $100,000 salary (~$8,333/month), that's about $2,333 for housing. Your down payment, interest rate, and existing debts adjust the final number.

It's a lending guideline: your monthly housing costs should stay under 28% of gross monthly income, and all debt payments (housing + car + student loans + credit cards) under 36%. Lenders use it to gauge how much mortgage you can safely carry.

home-affordability-calculator.q: Does my down payment change how much house I can afford?
home-affordability-calculator.a: Yes. A larger down payment means a smaller loan and lower monthly payment, so you can afford a higher purchase price. Putting down 20% or more also avoids private mortgage insurance (PMI), freeing up budget.

home-affordability-calculator.q: Should I include property taxes and insurance?
home-affordability-calculator.a: Yes — lenders count your full PITI payment (principal, interest, taxes, and insurance) against the 28% housing limit, so always include estimated property taxes and homeowners insurance when judging affordability.

loan-amortization-calculator.a: Amortization is the process of paying off a loan with fixed payments over time. Each payment covers interest first, then reduces the principal. Early payments are mostly interest; later payments are mostly principal.

loan-amortization-calculator.q: How is an amortization schedule calculated?
loan-amortization-calculator.a: The monthly payment is fixed using the loan amount, interest rate, and term. Each month, interest = balance × (annual rate ÷ 12), and the rest of the payment reduces the balance. Repeat until the balance reaches zero.

loan-amortization-calculator.q: Why is most of my early payment going to interest?
loan-amortization-calculator.a: Interest is charged on the outstanding balance, which is highest at the start. As you pay down principal, the interest portion shrinks and more of each payment goes toward the balance.

loan-amortization-calculator.q: How can I pay off an amortized loan faster?
loan-amortization-calculator.a: Make extra principal payments. Any amount above the scheduled payment goes straight to principal, which lowers future interest and shortens the loan term — often by years.

gpa-calculator.a: Convert each grade to points (A=4.0, B=3.0, C=2.0, D=1.0, F=0), multiply by the course's credit hours, add up the points, and divide by total credit hours. The result is your grade point average.

gpa-calculator.q: What is a weighted vs. unweighted GPA?
gpa-calculator.a: Unweighted GPA uses a 4.0 scale regardless of course difficulty. Weighted GPA adds extra points for honors or AP/IB classes (often up to 5.0), rewarding harder coursework.
A 3.0 is solid, 3.5+ is strong, and 3.7–4.0 is excellent and competitive for selective colleges and scholarships. Standards vary by school and program.

gpa-calculator.q: How do I raise my GPA?
gpa-calculator.a: Earn higher grades in high-credit courses, retake classes where your school replaces the grade, and balance difficult courses across terms. The more credit hours remaining, the more room to move your average.

net-worth-calculator.q: How do you calculate net worth?
net-worth-calculator.a: Net worth = total assets − total liabilities. Add up everything you own (cash, investments, home, car, retirement accounts) and subtract everything you owe (mortgage, loans, credit card balances).

net-worth-calculator.q: What counts as an asset vs. a liability?
net-worth-calculator.a: Assets are things of value you own: bank balances, investments, retirement accounts, real estate, and vehicles. Liabilities are debts you owe: mortgages, auto and student loans, and credit card balances.

net-worth-calculator.q: What is a good net worth?
net-worth-calculator.a: It depends on age and income. One common benchmark is the 'age × income ÷ 10' rule. More important than any single number is whether your net worth is growing year over year.
Yes. If your debts exceed your assets — common early in life with student loans or a new mortgage — net worth is negative. Paying down debt and building savings moves it positive over time.

debt-to-income-calculator.a: DTI is the share of your gross monthly income that goes to debt payments. DTI = total monthly debt payments ÷ gross monthly income × 100. It tells lenders how much of your income is already committed.

debt-to-income-calculator.q: What is a good DTI ratio?
debt-to-income-calculator.a: Lenders generally prefer a DTI of 36% or lower, with no more than 28% going to housing. Many mortgage programs allow up to 43%, and some up to 50% with strong credit and reserves.

debt-to-income-calculator.q: What debts are included in DTI?
debt-to-income-calculator.a: Include recurring monthly debt: mortgage or rent, auto loans, student loans, minimum credit card payments, and other loan payments. Utilities, groceries, and insurance are typically excluded.

debt-to-income-calculator.q: How can I lower my DTI?
debt-to-income-calculator.a: Pay down loan balances, avoid new debt, and increase income. Even paying off one small loan can meaningfully lower your ratio and improve loan approval odds.

savings-goal-calculator.q: How do I calculate how much to save each month?
savings-goal-calculator.a: Divide your goal by the number of months you have, then adjust for interest. For a $12,000 goal in 24 months, you'd need about $500/month before interest — less if your savings earn a return.

savings-goal-calculator.q: How does interest affect my savings goal?
savings-goal-calculator.a: Interest helps your money grow, so you need to contribute slightly less to reach the same goal. The higher the rate and the longer the timeframe, the bigger the boost from compounding.

savings-goal-calculator.q: Should I save in a high-yield savings account?
savings-goal-calculator.a: For short-term goals, yes — a high-yield savings account or money-market account earns more than a standard account while keeping your money safe and accessible.

savings-goal-calculator.q: What if I can't hit my monthly savings target?
savings-goal-calculator.a: Extend your timeline, lower the goal amount, or increase income. Automating transfers on payday makes consistent saving far more likely than relying on willpower.

percentage-calculator.q: How do you calculate a percentage of a number?
percentage-calculator.a: Multiply the number by the percentage as a decimal. 20% of 150 is 150 × 0.20 = 30. To find what percent one number is of another, divide and multiply by 100.

percentage-calculator.q: How do I calculate percentage increase or decrease?
percentage-calculator.a: Percent change = (new − old) ÷ old × 100. If a price rises from $80 to $100, that's (100 − 80) ÷ 80 × 100 = 25% increase.

percentage-calculator.q: What is X as a percentage of Y?
percentage-calculator.a: Divide X by Y and multiply by 100. For example, 45 out of 60 is 45 ÷ 60 × 100 = 75%.

percentage-calculator.q: How do I add or subtract a percentage from a number?
percentage-calculator.a: To add 15% to 200: 200 × 1.15 = 230. To subtract 15%: 200 × 0.85 = 170. Multiply by (1 + rate) to add or (1 − rate) to subtract.

paycheck-calculator.q: How is my take-home pay calculated?
paycheck-calculator.a: Take-home pay is your gross pay minus federal and state income tax withholding, Social Security (6.2%), Medicare (1.45%), and any pre-tax deductions like 401(k) and health insurance.

paycheck-calculator.q: What's the difference between gross and net pay?
paycheck-calculator.a: Gross pay is your salary before any deductions. Net pay (take-home) is what lands in your bank account after taxes and deductions are withheld.

paycheck-calculator.q: How much of my paycheck goes to taxes?
paycheck-calculator.a: It varies by income and location, but FICA alone takes 7.65% (Social Security + Medicare). Add federal and state income tax, and total withholding often ranges from about 15% to 35%.

paycheck-calculator.q: How can I increase my take-home pay?
paycheck-calculator.a: Adjust your W-4 withholding to match your actual tax liability, and review pre-tax deductions. Note that pre-tax 401(k) or HSA contributions lower taxable income, reducing tax even as they build savings.

salary-to-hourly-calculator.q: How do I convert my salary to an hourly rate?
salary-to-hourly-calculator.a: Divide your annual salary by the number of hours you work in a year. Hourly rate = salary ÷ (hours per week × weeks per year). At 40 hours/week and 52 weeks, that's salary ÷ 2,080.

salary-to-hourly-calculator.q: What is $60,000 a year per hour?
salary-to-hourly-calculator.a: About $28.85 per hour, based on a 40-hour week and 52 weeks ($60,000 ÷ 2,080 = $28.85).

salary-to-hourly-calculator.q: How many work hours are in a year?
salary-to-hourly-calculator.a: A standard full-time year is about 2,080 hours (40 hours × 52 weeks). If you take unpaid time off, use fewer weeks to get a more accurate hourly rate.

salary-to-hourly-calculator.q: Is the hourly rate before or after taxes?
salary-to-hourly-calculator.a: It's your gross (pre-tax) hourly rate. Your take-home rate will be lower after income tax, Social Security, Medicare, and other deductions.

hourly-to-salary-calculator.q: How do I convert hourly pay to an annual salary?
hourly-to-salary-calculator.a: Multiply your hourly rate by the hours you work per week and the weeks you work per year. Annual salary = rate × hours per week × weeks per year.
About $52,000 per year at 40 hours/week for 52 weeks ($25 × 40 × 52).
About $41,600 per year at 40 hours/week for 52 weeks ($20 × 40 × 52).

hourly-to-salary-calculator.q: Does this include taxes or benefits?
hourly-to-salary-calculator.a: No. The result is gross pay before taxes and deductions, and it doesn't include benefits like health insurance or retirement matching.

hours-calculator.q: How do I calculate hours worked?
hours-calculator.a: Subtract your start time from your end time, then subtract any break. From 9:00 AM to 5:00 PM with a 30-minute break, that's 8 hours − 0.5 = 7.5 hours.

hours-calculator.q: How do I convert minutes to decimal hours?
hours-calculator.a: Divide the minutes by 60. So 15 minutes = 0.25 hours, 30 minutes = 0.5 hours, and 45 minutes = 0.75 hours. Payroll systems use decimal hours.

hours-calculator.q: Does the calculator handle overnight shifts?
hours-calculator.a: Yes. If your end time is earlier than your start time, the calculator assumes the shift crosses midnight and counts the hours correctly.

hours-calculator.q: What is 7 hours and 30 minutes in decimal?
hours-calculator.a: 7.5 hours, because 30 minutes is half an hour (30 ÷ 60 = 0.5).

overtime-calculator.a: Multiply your hourly rate by the overtime multiplier to get the overtime rate, then multiply by overtime hours. Total pay = regular pay + overtime pay.

overtime-calculator.q: What is time and a half?
overtime-calculator.a: Time and a half means 1.5 times your regular hourly rate for overtime hours. At $20/hour, time and a half is $30/hour.

overtime-calculator.q: When am I entitled to overtime?
overtime-calculator.a: Under the US Fair Labor Standards Act, non-exempt employees earn at least 1.5× their regular rate for hours worked beyond 40 in a workweek. Some states add daily overtime rules.

overtime-calculator.q: What is $20 an hour at time and a half?
overtime-calculator.a: $30 per hour. Multiply $20 by 1.5 to get the overtime rate.

date-difference-calculator.q: How do I calculate the number of days between two dates?
date-difference-calculator.a: Subtract the earlier date from the later date. The formula is: Days = End Date − Start Date. For example, January 1 to March 31 spans 89 days (31 + 28 + 31 − 1). Our calculator handles leap years, month-length variations, and daylight-saving edge cases automatically.

date-difference-calculator.q: What is the difference between inclusive and exclusive day counting?
date-difference-calculator.a: Exclusive counting (the default) counts the gaps between dates — January 1 to January 3 = 2 days. Inclusive counting adds the end date itself — January 1 to January 3 = 3 days. Use inclusive when counting event nights (e.g., a 3-night hotel stay), contract days, or subscription days. Use exclusive for 'how many days until' countdowns.

date-difference-calculator.q: How many weeks are in a year?
date-difference-calculator.a: A calendar year has exactly 52 weeks and 1 day (365 days ÷ 7 = 52.14 weeks), or 52 weeks and 2 days in a leap year (366 days). That's why the same date shifts by one weekday each year (two days after a leap year).

date-difference-calculator.q: How do I count business days between two dates?
date-difference-calculator.a: Business days exclude weekends (Saturday and Sunday) and public holidays. To estimate: take the total calendar days, subtract the number of Saturdays and Sundays in the range, then subtract any holidays. Our business days calculator handles this automatically if you need precise working-day counts.

date-difference-calculator.q: How do I calculate someone's exact age in days?
date-difference-calculator.a: Enter the person's birth date as the start date and today's date as the end date, then click Calculate. The result is their exact age in total days, weeks, months, and years. For example, someone born on June 20, 2000, turns exactly 26 years old on June 20, 2026 — that's 9,497 days.

date-difference-calculator.q: Does daylight saving time affect the day count?
date-difference-calculator.a: No. This calculator uses UTC midnight timestamps for both dates, so 'spring forward' and 'fall back' clock changes do not affect the result. The answer is always the correct number of calendar days regardless of time zone.

fraction-calculator.q: How do you add fractions with different denominators?
fraction-calculator.a: To add fractions with different denominators, first find a common denominator by multiplying the two denominators together (or using the least common multiple). Then convert each fraction to an equivalent fraction with that common denominator, and add the numerators. Finally, simplify the result. Example: 1/2 + 1/3 = 3/6 + 2/6 = 5/6.
To multiply fractions, simply multiply the numerators together and the denominators together. Then simplify the result. Example: 2/3 × 3/4 = (2×3)/(3×4) = 6/12 = 1/2. No common denominator is needed.

To divide fractions, multiply the first fraction by the reciprocal (the flip) of the second fraction. Example: 2/3 ÷ 4/5 = 2/3 × 5/4 = 10/12 = 5/6. The phrase "keep, change, flip" helps: keep the first fraction, change ÷ to ×, flip the second fraction.

fraction-calculator.q: How do you calculate with mixed numbers like 2 3/4?
fraction-calculator.a: Convert the mixed number to an improper fraction first: multiply the whole number by the denominator and add the numerator. For 2 3/4: (2 × 4) + 3 = 11, so 2 3/4 = 11/4. Then perform the operation normally and convert back to a mixed number if needed.

A fraction is fully simplified (in lowest terms) when the numerator and denominator share no common factors other than 1. To simplify, divide both numerator and denominator by their Greatest Common Factor (GCF). Example: 6/12 simplified is 1/2, because GCF(6,12) = 6.

Subtracting fractions works just like addition: find a common denominator, convert each fraction, then subtract the numerators. Example: 3/4 − 1/3 = 9/12 − 4/12 = 5/12. If the result is negative, the first fraction was smaller than the second.

average-calculator.q: What is the difference between mean, median, and mode?
average-calculator.a: The mean (average) is the sum of all values divided by the count. The median is the middle value when numbers are sorted — it is less affected by outliers. The mode is the value that appears most often. For example, in [1, 2, 2, 3, 100]: mean = 21.6, median = 2, mode = 2. Use mean for symmetric data, median when outliers exist, mode for most-common-value questions.

average-calculator.q: How do I calculate the average of a set of numbers?
average-calculator.a: Add all the numbers together, then divide by how many numbers there are. Formula: Mean = Sum ÷ Count. Example: the average of 10, 20, and 30 is (10 + 20 + 30) ÷ 3 = 60 ÷ 3 = 20.

average-calculator.q: What if my data set has no mode?
average-calculator.a: If every number in the set appears exactly once (no repeats), there is no mode. This is normal — not every data set has a mode. Some data sets are bimodal (two modes) or multimodal (more than two), which means multiple values tie for the highest frequency.

average-calculator.q: How is the median calculated when there is an even number of values?
average-calculator.a: When the count is even, sort the values and average the two middle ones. Example: for [3, 7, 9, 13], the two middle values are 7 and 9, so the median is (7 + 9) ÷ 2 = 8. This guarantees the median is always between the two center values.

average-calculator.q: Why is median salary used instead of average salary?
average-calculator.a: Salary data is right-skewed — a small number of very high earners pull the mean upward. For example, if 9 workers earn $50,000 and 1 earns $500,000, the mean is $95,000 but the median is $50,000. The median better represents what a typical worker earns.

average-calculator.q: Can I calculate the average of negative numbers?
average-calculator.a: Yes. The mean formula works the same for negative numbers. Example: the average of -10, -5, 0, 5, 10 is (-10 + -5 + 0 + 5 + 10) ÷ 5 = 0 ÷ 5 = 0. This calculator fully supports negative values and decimals.
